review_comments.author: Nhat Pham
review_comments.summary: Identified a zswap shrinker bug that causes eviction shadows to be lost, leading to refault detection issues. Proposed a patch to fix this by saving the existing shadow and handing it to zswap.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Thu, Jul 23, 2026 at 6:39 AM Johannes Weiner <hannes@cmpxchg.org> wrote:
>
> On Sat, Jul 18, 2026 at 11:36:39AM +0200, Alexandre Ghiti wrote:
>
> I just chatted with Alexandre off-list, but this path does not store
> an eviction shadow like the reclaim path does. That means no refaults
> are detected from dropbehind pages, which can fundamentally alter
> reclaim decisions, zswap shrinker force, and pressure detection.

I think it's a zswap shrinker bug even before this patch. When we
allocate a new buffer for the writeback request and add to swap cache,
we erase the old shadow :(

I had a patch sometimes ago to fix this - basically, what it did is
when we allocate the buffer for writeback and add it to swap cache, we
save the existing shadow and hand that shadow to zswap. zswap will
store it in the zswap tree tree (in place of the struct zswap entry
that is being freed).

Now you will have to juggle it a bit in various path:

1. swapin has to check the zswap tree for refault.

2. remove_mapping does not create a new shadow, but instead restore
the zswap's owned shadow, if there is one.

3. zswap operations have to now handle shadows stored in its trees too.

I think there is one edge case where we still lose the old shadow (I
need to check), but the rest seems to work.

It's a bit complex, yes, but I managed to find more uses for this
added complexity too. With this, I was able to add more monitoring to
zswap shrinker - you can track how many shadows are being stored in
the zswap tree, which gives you a per-cgroup counter of inflight zswap
buffers. This helps with diagnosing when zswap writeback is generating
too much memory pressure with these buffers. You can also use this
counter it for zswap writeback heuristics (for instance, throttle
writeback when there are too many inflight buffers already and we're
not freeing them fast enough).

I have not had the chance to make it upstream-ready, given all of my
attention being taken away by vswap and other endeavors, and the fact
that swap architecture has changed quite a bit.

review_comments.author: Nhat Pham
review_comments.summary: Further elaborated on the zswap shrinker bug, explaining how it affects workingset tracking heuristics and refault detection. Mentioned a potential edge case where the old shadow is still lost.
review_comments.raw_body:
On Thu, Jul 23, 2026 at 8:38 AM Nhat Pham <nphamcs@gmail.com> wrote:
>
> >
> > I just chatted with Alexandre off-list, but this path does not store
> > an eviction shadow like the reclaim path does. That means no refaults
> > are detected from dropbehind pages, which can fundamentally alter
> > reclaim decisions, zswap shrinker force, and pressure detection.
>
> I think it's a zswap shrinker bug even before this patch. When we
> allocate a new buffer for the writeback request and add to swap cache,
> we erase the old shadow :(

... and count it as a refault :| Which is even worse than just silent
erasure. It throws off all our workingset tracking heuristics,
inaccurately reporting refault on a really cold page (writeback
candidate).

that change separately, mainly out of abundance of caution as
writeback is done synchronously here so the extra latency could be

review_comments.summary: Author addressed Yosry Ahmed's concern about the arbitrary global limit of 64 vs cgroup limit of 1, explaining that batching is consistent with direct reclaim and cgroup limit reclaim, but prefers to start with a clean and consistent version rather than introducing an inconsistency.
review_comments.raw_body:
It's not an unknown, right? We know this works for direct reclaimers,
cgroup limit reclaim e.g., and what the latency implications are.

Because of how reclaim works, we also know it'll call zswap_store() in
batches of SWAP_CLUSTER_MAX. If we don't batch here, they're likely to
each call shrink_memcg() once we're at the limit - while still risking
rejections due to compressibility differences.

My worry is that if we start with an inconsistency, we'll be stuck
with it for a long time.

I'd rather start with the clean, consistent version. Dial it back only
if we have data to justfiy the complication that we can put into a

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 01/11] mm: add PMD swap entry detection support
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds support for detecting PMD (Page Map Directory) swap entries in the Linux kernel. Currently, when a huge page is swapped out, it's split into smaller pages, but this patch preserves the huge page information by recognizing and handling PMD-level swap entries instead of PTE-level ones. The patch introduces new helpers to manage exclusive overlay bits for PMD softleaf entries on various architectures and provides no-op fallbacks for unsupported architectures.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 02/11] mm: add PMD swap entry splitting support
series_items.patch_summary: This patch series adds support for splitting PMD swap entries into 512 PTE swap entries in the Linux kernel's memory management (mm) subsystem. The goal is to improve performance and efficiency when handling large pages and swap operations.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 03/11] mm: handle PMD swap entries in fork path
series_items.patch_summary: This patch improves the handling of PMD swap entries in the fork path by mirroring the behavior of copy_nonpresent_pte(). It introduces a new function, swap_dup_entries_direct(), which can duplicate a contiguous range of swap slots. The patch also adds a retry mechanism for table allocation failures and preserves the THP (Transparent Huge Pages) during the fork process.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 04/11] mm: zswap: add range lookup for large-folio swapin
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a range lookup feature to the zswap compression algorithm, allowing it to handle large folios by scanning the covered range and checking if any slots are still in zswap. If no slot is found, it returns -ENOENT so the caller can read from the backing device. If any slot is present, it fails the large-folio read to prevent returning partially initialized data. The patch also introduces a new function `zswap_is_present` to enable PMD swap-entry consumers to make range decisions before attempting PMD-order swapin.

review_comments.author: Usama Arif (author)
review_comments.summary:
Author acknowledged a potential race condition in the zswap implementation when handling large-folio swapin and PMD swap entries, explained that the correct action would be to reject the PMD order read and fall back to per-page loading, but expressed uncertainty about how to handle this situation due to the size of the series.

Author acknowledged that their initial version of the patch would stop working if zswap was ever enabled, and asked for suggestions from Yosry Ahmed on what his preference is for zswap.
review_comments.raw_body:
Yes, so we can end up in a race, which should be handled gracefully.

For example, lets say we have zswap writeback enabled, which means we
can end up in a state where we have a PMD swap entry and 511 of the 512
slots have been written to disk, but 1 slot (slot X) is still in zswap.

We can then have the following race:


  CPU A: PMD swap-in                  CPU B: split-PTE swap-in
  ------------------                 ------------------------

  Checks swap cache: empty

                                       Faults slot X
                                       Adds order-0 folio F
                                       zswap_load(F):
                                         removes X from zswap
                                         marks F dirty

  Checks zswap range: empty
  A is preempted

                                       Unmaps/reclaims F
                                       zswap_store(F):
                                         puts X back in zswap
                                       Removes F from swap cache

  A resumes
  Allocates large swap-cache folio G
  zswap_load(G) finds X in zswap



The correct action would be to reject the PMD order read and fall back
to per-page loading.

I am bit torn about what to do for zswap here. The series is quite big
already. Alexandre is looking at adding support for PMD swap, but will
send patches once this series gets merged. My initial versions 1
and 2, basically stopped installing PMD swap entry if zswap was ever enabled.

---

so that PMD swap entry can keep on working.

Both of these paths are temporary till Alex sends his series. I
do feel my initial version was simpler but will basically stop
working if zswap is ever enabled. Do you have any suggestions on
what your preference is for zswap?

Thanks!
Usama

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 05/11] mm: swap in PMD swap entries as whole THPs during swapoff
series_items.patch_summary: This patch improves the handling of PMD-level swap entries during swapoff by allowing them to be swapped in as whole THPs (Transparent Huge Pages). It introduces a new function, `swap_pmd_cache_lookup`, which classifies the swap cache behind a PMD swap entry as empty, backed by one PMD-sized folio, or requiring per-page handling. The patch also adds functions to unuse PMD-level swap entries and split them into PTE-level entries if necessary. This change mirrors the existing behavior for PTE-level entries and preserves soft-dirty, exclusive, and UFFD (User Fault) state when installing the present PMD.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 06/11] mm: handle PMD swap entries in non-present PMD walkers
series_items.patch_summary: This patch updates various non-present PMD walkers in the Linux kernel to handle swap entries correctly, mirroring their PTE-level equivalents. It introduces a new helper function `smaps_account_swap()` and modifies several functions such as `move_soft_dirty_pmd()`, `clear_soft_dirty_pmd()`, and `pagemap_pmd_range_thp()` to account for swap entries alongside migration entries. The patch also fixes issues with MADV_FREE, MADV_COLD, and MADV_PAGEOUT operations on swapped-out THPs, ensuring that swap slots are properly handled and released.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 07/11] mm: handle PMD swap entries in MADV_WILLNEED
series_items.patch_summary: This patch improves handling of PMD (page map directory) swap entries in the MADV_WILLNEED operation. It ensures that when a PMD swap entry is encountered, it is properly split and handled by the PTE path if necessary. The patch also optimizes the prefetching of PMD-sized folios to avoid unnecessary splitting and improves handling of zswap entries. This enhances the overall performance and reliability of the Linux kernel's memory management system.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 08/11] mm: handle PMD swap entries in UFFDIO_MOVE
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds support for moving PMD-level swap entries in the UFFDIO_MOVE operation. It introduces a new function, move_swap_pmd(), which moves the swap entry whole-PMD and re-anchors the PMD-sized swap-cache folio's anon rmap to the destination VMA. The patch also updates the dispatcher in move_pages_huge_pmd() to wait for migration on PMD migration entries and route PMD swap entries through move_swap_pmd(). This allows for more efficient movement of large pages, reducing the need for splitting via the PTE path.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 09/11] mm: handle PMD swap entry faults on swap-in
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a new function do_huge_pmd_swap_page() to handle faults on PMD (2MB) swap entries during swap-in operations. It resolves the entire mapping in one shot, mirroring the behavior of do_swap_page() for PTEs. The handler checks if the folio is already in the swap cache and allocates a new PMD-order folio if not. It also handles cases where the folio has been split or removed from the cache due to racing swap-in operations or memory failures. Additionally, it refuses to map folios containing hardware-poisoned subpages and restores soft_dirty and uffd_wp flags as needed.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 10/11] mm: install PMD swap entries on swap-out
series_items.patch_summary: This patch optimizes the swap-out process for huge pages (Transparent Huge Pages, or THPs) by installing PMD-level swap entries instead of splitting them into individual PTEs. This reduces overhead and preserves the huge mapping across the swap round-trip. The patch mirrors the existing PTE swap-out path at PMD granularity, handling zswap and adjusting MM_ANONPAGES / MM_SWAPENTS accordingly. It also introduces a new counter, thp_swpout_pmd, to track PMD mappings replaced by PMD-level swap entries.

Is there a reason we can't do that?

series_items.subject: [PATCH v5 11/11] selftests/mm: add PMD swap entry tests
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds a set of tests to exercise the PMD swap entry paths in the Linux kernel. The tests cover various scenarios, including swapping out and in THPs (Transparent Huge Pages), fork and write operations, userfaultfd, munmap, mprotect, mremap, pagemap reading, mincore, MADV_FREE, MADV_WILLNEED, and swapoff. The tests are designed to verify the correctness of data and the behavior of PMD-mapped THPs in different configurations, including with and without zswap enabled. The patch also adds a new category 'pmd_swap' and a wrapper script 'ksft_pmd_swap.sh' to run the tests.

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch series introduces PMD-level swap entries for anonymous THPs, allowing huge mappings to survive the swap round-trip without needing khugepaged to collapse the range later. The PMD swap entry is a compact page-table encoding for HPAGE_PMD_NR consecutive swap slots and does not promise that the swap cache always contains one PMD-sized folio. When reclaim swaps out a PMD-mapped anonymous THP, it can now restore the PMD mapping directly on swap-in without waiting for khugepaged to collapse the range later. The series includes 11 patches that add support for PMD swap entry detection, splitting, and handling in various paths such as fork, zswap, and swap. This change aims to improve performance by reducing the overhead of split-THP and khugepaged.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v4 1/2] mm/vmstat, mm/memcontrol: add _monotonic vmstat readers
series_items.patch_summary: This patch adds monotonic versions of vmstat readers to handle monotonically-incremented event counters correctly across signed-long wraparound. The existing clamped helpers are kept for non-monotonic page-count readers, which can still present transient negative reads as zero. The new _monotonic variants return the underlying state value cast to unsigned long, ensuring correct results when computing deltas between two samples. This patch is a prerequisite for replacing producer-side accumulators with read-side accumulators that sample monotonic per-LRU vmstat counters.

series_items.subject: [PATCH v4 2/2] mm/vmscan: reduce lru_lock contention via vmstat-derived scan-balance cost
series_items.patch_summary: This patch reduces contention on the lru_lock by moving the calculation of scan balance costs from being accumulated under the lock to using per-LRU vmstat counters. The old approach was causing contention and overhead due to frequent lock acquisitions, especially in memory-heavy workloads. The new approach uses read-side accumulators fed from these counters, which are already incremented by various reclaim operations. This change should improve performance on systems with high memory usage.

review_comments.author: Johannes Weiner
review_comments.summary:
The reviewer, Johannes Weiner, suggested simplifying the patch description by focusing on the key idea of deriving the balance formula from vmstat counters instead of recording costs directly, which would reduce lock contention and make the code cheaper.

Gave Acked-by
review_comments.raw_body:
^^^

This is a quite a bit of explaining the code, which is kind of
drowning out what's really going on. How about something like:

---

The balance formula for anon and file, respectively, is this:

    cost = nr_io * SWAP_CLUSTER_MAX + nr_rotated

Instead of recording cost and running averaging logic directly when
these events occur, snapshot running vmstat counters once per reclaim
cycle and derive the balance from event deltas since the last run.

WORKINGSET_RESTORE_* and NR_VMSCAN_WRITE already exist and have been
accounted all along; PGROTATE_* are added for replacing the rotation
event callbacks.

This is overall cheaper and has fewer lock aquisition sites.

---

review_comments.author: Shakeel Butt
review_comments.summary: Reviewer noted that exposing pgrotate_[anon|file] metrics to userspace may cause confusion due to their different meanings compared to older and newer metrics, and suggested delaying userspace exposure until a solid use case is established.
review_comments.raw_body:
Oh man too long commit message, please follow Johannes's suggestion.

The patch looks good but I have one question. We already have concept of
pgrotated through /proc/vmstat which represents number of pages we have moved to
the tail of inactive LRU to make them next reclaim candidates. This patch is
exposing pgrotate_[anon|file] to userspace. Here these metrics represents either
the number of pages we scanned but didn't reclaim plus number of pages given one
more trip in the active LRU.

Older and newer pgrotate metrics kind of represent something different. It may
cause confusion. Now before suggesting to change the name, let me ask do we
really need to expose these to the userspace? How exactly users can use these
new metrics? I think you can use them here without exposing to userspace. So,
does it makes sense to delay userspace exposure later when we have a more solid
usecase for that unless you have it already.

BTW really awesome work and I really like how these patches have reduced the lru
lock contention drastically.

version_history.subject: [PATCH] fs: push nr_cached_objects memcg gating into individual filesystems
[PATCH v2] fs: push nr_cached_objects memcg gating into individual filesystems

patches_submitted.patch_summary: This patch moves the check for whether a filesystem's nr_cached_objects hook is memcg-aware from fs/super.c to individual filesystems, allowing each filesystem to handle its own memcg awareness independently. The patch introduces a helper function mem_cgroup_shrink_is_root() in <linux/memcontrol.h> to simplify this check. This change fixes an issue where the assumption that non-root memcgs are not aware of nr_cached_objects was incorrect for XFS, which intentionally drives inode reclaim from per-memcg contexts. The patch also removes a gate in fs/super.c and adds support for XFS's inode-reclaim hook to be driven from per-memcg contexts.

patches_reviewed.subject: Re: [PATCH v5 04/11] mm: zswap: add range lookup for large-folio swapin
patches_reviewed.patch_summary: This patch addresses a potential race condition in the zswap system, which can occur when zswap writeback is enabled and a PMD swap entry is being loaded. The issue arises when one CPU thread is loading a large-folio swap-in while another thread is splitting a PTE (page table entry) and adding it to the zswap cache. To resolve this, the patch adds range lookup for large-folio swapin, ensuring that if the PMD swap entry is partially loaded, the correct action is taken by rejecting the PMD order read and falling back to per-page loading.

review_comments.author: Yosry Ahmed
review_comments.summary:
Reviewer suggested checking the zswap range after allocating a folio in the swap cache, assuming the folio is already locked and the result will be stable

Reviewer pointed out that even with PMD zswap load support, the issue of order-0 pages being in zswap and some on disk still exists, which would lead to a race condition.
review_comments.raw_body:
Why don't we check the zswap range after allocating a folio in the
swap cache? I am assuming at this point we have the folio locked and
the result should be stable?

---

Even with PMD zswap load support, we still have to deal with the case
where some order-0 pages are in zswap and some are on disk, right? I
don't see how that would eliminate the case you mentioned above.

review_comments.author: Nhat Pham
review_comments.summary: Reviewer suggested performing the check in __swap_cache_add_check() to prevent a potential race condition between PMD swap-in and split-PTE swap-in, citing a similar approach used for vswap.
review_comments.raw_body:
+1. Is it possible to perform the check in __swap_cache_add_check?

That's what I did for vswap, per Kairui's suggestions. The cluster
lock is held from that point all the way to after the folio is
locked-and-loaded into swap cache, at which point no one can change
the backend under you.
